takeaway: NN/g defines a user interview as "a research method where the interviewer asks participants questions about a topic, listens to their responses, and follows up with further questions to learn more"; the core skills are open-ended questions, rapport, and genuine listening.

Steve Portigal's Interviewing Users argues that interviewing is a learnable skill, not an innate talent, and that the goal is moving "from simply gathering data to uncovering powerful insights about people."

Teresa Torres warns that some question types "let people answer with their ideal version of themselves rather than a realistic portrayal of their everyday behavior," so ask about specific past stories; she frames weekly interviewing as a keystone habit.

Rob Fitzpatrick's The Mom Test teaches you to ask about the customer's actual life and past behavior instead of pitching, because people lie politely about hypotheticals; "it's not a real sales lead until you've given them such a concrete chance to reject you."

Recruiting the right participants is what makes findings valid; screeners must hide the study's intent because "since all questions in a written screener are typically visible upfront, participants may guess the purpose of the study and tailor their responses to fit."

Good screening questions face two conflicting goals: "They must elicit specific information about users. They should also avoid revealing specific information about the study" — solved with open-ended wording or realistic distractor answers.

An interview guide is a flexible plan for a semi-structured interview: "A good interview guide paves the way for a deep, free-flowing conversation with participants," pairing warmup rapport questions with an ordered set of research topics.

A leading question includes or implies the desired answer in its phrasing, so participants echo the interviewer instead of sharing real experience — producing, in NN/g's words, "biased or false answers."

"Open-ended questions allow participants to give a free-form text answer," surfacing surprises, while closed questions limit answers to fixed options; the funnel technique opens broad and narrows to specifics to avoid priming.

The Mom Test's three rules are: talk about their life, not your idea; ask about specifics in the past, not opinions about the future; and talk less, listen more — its foundational line is "Talk about their life, not your idea."

The funnel technique means you "start with broad, open-ended questions in your interviews before introducing more specific questions to gather accurate, in-depth information," so you don't bias early answers with narrow framing.
Probing means following up to draw out detail with open prompts like "tell me more" and "why," turning a thin first answer into the richer context behind it.

NN/g's five interview-facilitation mistakes are weak rapport, too little probing, multitasking, letting observers disrupt, and leading via questions or body language; note that "participants will often 'mirror' the researcher, so speaking slowly will help them remain calm as well."

Indi Young's listening sessions aim to understand a person's interior thinking rather than run a question list: "We're just there to understand that person. We don't have a list of questions. We don't have a clock running."
Affinity diagramming "refers to organizing related observations, ideas, concepts, or findings into distinct clusters," turning a pile of interview notes into themes the team builds consensus around.

Thematic analysis systematically codes segments of qualitative data to surface recurring patterns; it "renders important aspects of qualitative data visible and makes uncovering themes easier" across many participants.
